pageTitle.value: Anne Lacaton & Arine Aprahamian - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: Young Lebanese-Armenian architect Arine Aprahamian is inspired to create buildings that are innovative, affordable and sustainable. Her philosophy sits well with French Pritzker-Prize winning architect Anne Lacaton, who espouses building renewal over demolition.

WaPageTitle.value: Anne Lacaton & Arine Aprahamian - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: Young architect Arine Aprahamian is collaborating with mentor Anne Lacaton on a visionary project to revitalize Beirut's Bourj Hammoud quarter. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Anne Lacaton & Arine Aprahamian - Rolex mentoring programme

text.value: Young Lebanese-Armenian architect Arine Aprahamian is inspired to create buildings that are innovative, affordable and sustainable. Her philosophy sits well with French Pritzker-Prize winning architect Anne Lacaton, who espouses building renewal over demolition. During their two-year collaboration, Aprahamian visited Lacaton in Paris many times to discuss a theoretical project on the reintegration of abandoned buildings in the crowded Bourj Hammoud quarter of Beirut. Here, they each relate how they experienced their relationship.

“I grew up in Bourj Hammoud, a densely populated area in the eastern suburbs of Beirut. As a child, I daydreamed about redesigning my bedroom, rearranging my home, and fantasized about the possibilities and kinds of space I would like to be in. This spatial instinct drove me towards architecture.

For years after graduating, I always longed for mentorship, but could never have imagined such an opportunity. It’s extremely important to have a mentor – the relationship always brings fresh ideas. It’s been a wonderful experience with Anne Lacaton. We agree that to practise architecture, you need a set of core values to guide you. We constantly speak about the value of the ‘existing’, especially as construction is one of the most polluting and extractive sectors in the world.

For our collaboration Anne was intent that the project be in Bourj Hammoud. The point was to find derelict or unused spaces that have great architectural and spatial value, and to think about how to bring them back to life – not only theoretically, but practically and financially, and to do the full exercise as if it were real.

After an analysis of the neighbourhood, I chose some abandoned buildings, with the goal of finding a way to reactivate and reintegrate them into the urban and social fabric. To discuss these things with an architect of Anne’s calibre is a big drive towards taking the project in the right direction... You never know, it may actually happen one day.

Bourj Hammoud is a grid of really small plots, and really tight streets, with very scarce public or communal spaces and limited resources. It is densely and fully developed with only the odd empty plot. But if you look closely, you notice all these small, empty pockets of liminal space in between the buildings. One of the goals of the project is to organize blocks of buildings to work with and enhance these spaces for people to enjoy.

However, there is a hierarchy of intervention. You can’t start talking about public programming or aesthetics for instance if there’s no power, or if you are water-insecure in your home. The primary goal is to offer functionality and flexibility; maximum adaptability with the minimum possible resources and intervention. It could be as simple as bringing back services to a building. The point being to give a building life again and to allow it enough flexibility to adapt and transform based on the needs of the community. You learn that space is luxury and that even minor manipulation creates a big impact in places where it may seem too chaotic to make an improvement.”

title.value: However, there is a hierarchy of intervention. You can’t start talking about public programming or aesthetics for instance if there’s no power, or if you are water-insecure in your home.

text.value:
“What interested me in this mentorship was the exchange. I am still curious to learn and Arine’s project in Beirut has put me in an unknown situation where I have to learn, understand, observe. It’s interesting to know how younger people – especially those who come from other cultures – approach similar subjects such as the ‘existing’.

Arine’s deep conviction about what she wanted to do and where she wanted to do it made an impression on me. Her project is based on Bourj Hammoud in Beirut where her family lives. I visited her there. I was impressed by her knowledge of this place. It is a neighbourhood that she loves, and she is deeply involved in its renewal.

The topic of her research is to work with what already exists. This is fundamental to my practice with Jean-Philippe Vassal. For us, not to demolish the buildings, not to cut trees – to let people stay in their buildings if they want to – it’s a rule. It is important to start from what exists and transform it – working with its stories, structures and uses – than always starting from nothing.

What is most important is to get to know the values of a place, because we must not lose them. In our way of working with what exists, we first identify these values so as to preserve them carefully, then correct what is not going well, and replace what is not good, but with great, great discernment.

Our essential approach to architecture is to think about it from the inside, from the inhabited space. It requires navigation between intuition, emotions, in parallel with scientific and technical work. These two approaches must work together until little by little they intersect and merge. And there we can begin – the project begins to take shape. To start, I recommended Arine create an inventory of all the spaces to survey everything she could see and perceive: the light, heights, relationships between spaces, or with the outside, whether the structure is open or compartmentalized, etc.

title.value: I don’t teach her much about how to do things; she has the tools. I have tried to push her towards an approach, which also pushes me to question my own way of doing things.

text.value:
I don’t teach her much about how to do things; she has the tools. I have tried to push her towards an approach, which also pushes me to question my own way of doing things. Sometimes I say to her: go back, look to the right, look to the left. Look carefully again. Sometimes I also bring references, examples that I know that can fuel reflection.

Arine is impressive. She is extremely courageous and works hard. She faces it all head on – she does not avoid any questions. I have sometimes tried to get her to take a step back, to escape (get away) from the analysis and to start generating ideas to stimulate her research. New parameters, new ideas, can disrupt analytical work but open up the project enormously. For me, the design process must remain open until the end.”

pageCaption.value: Young Filipino filmmaker Rafael Manuel collaborated for two years with acclaimed Chinese director Jia Zhang-Ke through the Rolex mentoring programme.

pageTitle.value: Bernardine Evaristo & Ayesha Harruna Attah - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: After finishing her studies in the US, author Ayesha Harruna Attah returned to Africa with a mission to write African stories for African readers. Five books later, she is ambitious for a wider readership.

WaPageTitle.value: Bernardine Evaristo & Ayesha Harruna Attah - Rolex mentoring programme

pageTitle.value: Dianne Reeves & Song Yi Jeon - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: For Grammy-winning jazz singer Dianne Reeves, the best way to mentor South Korean jazz composer and singer Song Yi Jeon was to invite her to perform with her band.
